text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Alex Carter  
King's College Hospital  
Department of Neurosciences, Denmark Hill, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr Alex Carter,  

Samuel Davies, DOB: 14-03-1983, Hospital No: KCH427119 NHS No. 9462017358  
12 Brookfield Mews, London, SE15 4QT

Thank you for asking me to review your patient in the neurology clinic. He attended today with his partner and provided a clear chronology. There is a remote history of a mild traumatic brain injury in early adulthood without documented loss of consciousness beyond a few minutes and no acute intracranial complications recorded at the time. He has not reported persistent neurological deficits from that episode. 

Regarding current events, he describes two patterns. He experiences focal sensory occur several times each week, particularly in the evenings. Generalised convulsions seizures are rare, typically five events per year. The sensory episodes are described as a brief rising paraesthesia starting in the left forearm with a metallic taste, lasting under one minute, and without clear impairment of awareness. The rare convulsive events have no consistent precipitant; recovery is said to be within 30–60 minutes, with fatigue and myalgia thereafter. His partner confirms scattered tongue biting and occasional urinary incontinence with the convulsive events. He does not drive. 

He previously trialled levetiracetam and lamotrigine at standard doses but discontinued these due to mood irritability and rash respectively. He is currently not on regular anti-seizure medication and uses a seizure diary app; entries over the past three months align with the frequencies above. Sleep deprivation and missed meals are noted by the patient as potential aggravators. There is no clear diurnal pattern beyond the evening clustering of the sensory episodes. No history of prolonged convulsive activity or status epilepticus was provided. 

Neurological examination in clinic today was unremarkable. Cardiovascular observations were stable. There are no focal deficits. Routine bloods from your practice last month (FBC, U&E, LFT, calcium, glucose, B12/folate, TSH) were reported as within reference limits. He has never had prolonged EEG monitoring. A prior standard EEG five years ago was reported as non-diagnostic; brain MRI from the same period was structurally normal on the report available to us. 

We discussed options for further characterisation of his events and the potential benefits of updated imaging and EEG. He is open to non-urgent repeat MRI with an epilepsy protocol and outpatient video-EEG, and he understands that results may guide future management decisions. He prefers to defer any medication changes until investigations are completed, which I think is reasonable given current stability between convulsive events and his prior adverse effects. We also reviewed safety, seizure first aid, and the importance of maintaining a regular sleep-wake routine. He will continue to keep a detailed diary noting time of day, possible triggers, and recovery features. 

I will arrange: 
- MRI brain (epilepsy protocol) 
- Outpatient video-EEG with activation procedures as tolerated 
- Follow-up in clinic once investigations are available, or sooner if the pattern changes 

Thank you for your ongoing care of this patient. Please let me know if there are any concerns in the meantime. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: He experiences focal sensory occur several times each week
focal sensory occur several times each week, particularly in the evenings. Generalised convulsions seizures are rare, typically five events per year

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Identify explicit frequencies: The letter states two seizure patterns. 1) Focal sensory episodes: 'several times each week' (vague but clearly multiple per week). 2) Generalised convulsions: 'typically five events per year'. Apply rule to choose highest frequency across types: multiple per week is more frequent than five per year. Therefore, normalize to 'multiple per week' since exact number is not specified. Not seizure-free. Provide supporting quote.
seizure_frequency_number: He experiences focal sensory occur several times each week
reference: focal sensory occur several times each week, particularly in the evenings. Generalised convulsions seizures are rare, typically five events per year
